stem: You are the GP seeing a 15-year-old girl who reports heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche at age 12. Her cycles last 8 to 10 days, flood through tampons and pads every two hours on the heaviest days, and she has missed school each cycle. She is sexually inactive and uses no contraception. She has not had previous investigations. She has otherwise been well, plays netball weekly and has no history of bruising, epistaxis or family bleeding disorders. Her mother is in the room and is supportive.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 16, T 36.6°C, weight 58 kg
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no thyroid enlargement, no abdominal mass, abdomen soft and non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, MCV 70 fL, ferritin 8 micrograms/L, beta-hCG negative, normal coagulation screen

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological therapy?

options.text: Endometrial ablation booked through paediatric gynaecology after consent from parents
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is contraindicated in adolescents and women who may want future fertility and is never first-line in this age group. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Oral combined ethinyloestradiol and levonorgestrel pill cycled monthly with iron supplementation
options.rationale: Combined oral contraceptive pills are a reasonable second-line option, but tranexamic acid is preferred as a first hormone-free trial in adolescents and avoids VTE risk discussions when not needed. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Insertion of a levonorgestrel intrauterine system under general anaesthesia within four weeks
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS is effective for HMB but is invasive in a sexually inactive 15-year-old and is reserved for failed first-line non-invasive therapy or contraceptive need. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Oral norethisterone 5 mg three times daily continuously from day 5 to day 26 of each cycle
options.rationale: Cyclical norethisterone in this regimen has lower efficacy than tranexamic acid for ovulatory HMB and is mostly used as a short bridging or rescue therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses with iron supplementation between cycles
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid reduces menstrual blood loss by approximately 40% and is the preferred first-line for adolescents who want to avoid hormonal therapy or systemic side effects; iron addresses the deficiency. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding; eTG: Gynaecology)

explanation.summary: Adolescent HMB is most often anovulatory cycling related to immature hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, but iron deficiency anaemia and inherited bleeding disorders such as von Willebrand disease should be considered. RANZCOG first-line is tranexamic acid for women not requiring contraception; combined oral contraceptive pills are second-line. Iron replacement, ideally oral with vitamin C, is added when ferritin is under 30 micrograms/L. Persistent symptoms despite first-line therapy prompt referral and consideration of LNG-IUS or further investigation.
key_points: Tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses cuts blood loss by ~40%.
Iron should be replaced when ferritin is under 30 micrograms/L.
Bleeding disorder screening is indicated when HMB starts at menarche.
explanation.pearls: In an adolescent with HMB since menarche, ferritin under 30 micrograms/L and no contraceptive need, start tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily for the first 4 days of menses plus oral iron.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar in the Day Assessment Unit reviewing a 34+0 weeks gravida 1 para 0 woman who self-presents with 24 hours of reduced fetal movements. She has had no antepartum bleeding, no rupture of membranes and no contractions. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to date with normal screening and a normal 20-week morphology scan. A growth scan two weeks ago showed an estimated fetal weight on the 30th centile with a normal umbilical artery Doppler. She is anxious and exhausted.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, RR 16, T 36.7°C, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Symphysio-fundal height 32 cm, longitudinal lie, cephalic presentation, fetal heart rate 142 bpm by auscultation
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 145 bpm, variability 4 bpm for 50 of 60 minutes, no accelerations, one shallow late deceleration after a Braxton-Hicks tightening

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange immediate ultrasound assessment of growth, liquor volume and umbilical artery Doppler within 4 hours
options.rationale: Reduced fetal movements at 34 weeks with a non-reactive CTG and a late deceleration warrant urgent ultrasound to identify growth restriction, oligohydramnios or abnormal Doppler that would change delivery timing. (Source: RANZCOG: Decreased fetal movements)

options.text: Admit for repeat CTG in 4 hours, maternal hydration and oral mobilisation without further investigation
options.rationale: Repeat CTG without addressing the abnormal current trace delays response to a potentially compromised fetus; ultrasound is needed in parallel. (Source: RANZCOG: Decreased fetal movements)

options.text: Discharge with kick chart, advice to return if movements decrease further and obstetric review in 7 days
options.rationale: Reduced fetal movements with reduced CTG variability and a late deceleration are concerning and require urgent escalation rather than community follow-up. (Source: RANZCOG: Decreased fetal movements)

options.text: Proceed directly to category 1 caesarean section within 30 minutes for non-reassuring fetal status
options.rationale: The CTG abnormalities, while concerning, are not in themselves category 1 indications and delivery decisions at 34 weeks need ultrasound and senior obstetric input. (Source: RANZCOG: Decreased fetal movements)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg intramuscularly and observe overnight with intermittent auscultation
options.rationale: Betamethasone is appropriate if preterm delivery is being planned, but intermittent auscultation alone is inadequate surveillance when the CTG already shows reduced variability and a late deceleration. (Source: RANZCOG: Decreased fetal movements)

explanation.summary: Reduced fetal movements at or after 28 weeks warrant urgent CTG and clinical assessment, with ultrasound used to evaluate growth, liquor and Doppler if the CTG is abnormal, growth has not been recently assessed or risk factors are present. Reduced variability beyond 40 minutes raises concern for fetal compromise. Antenatal corticosteroids are given if delivery before 34+6 weeks is anticipated. Multidisciplinary obstetric review guides timing and mode of delivery.
key_points: Urgent CTG is first-line; ultrasound is added when the CTG is abnormal or growth is unconfirmed.
Reduced variability for over 40 minutes is a warning sign of fetal hypoxia.
Antenatal steroids cover preterm delivery up to 34+6 weeks.
explanation.pearls: Reduced fetal movements plus a CTG with under 5 bpm variability for over 40 minutes triggers same-day ultrasound for growth, liquor and umbilical artery Doppler.

stem: You are the GP seeing a 28-year-old nulliparous woman who presents with three days of lower abdominal pain, vaginal discharge and dyspareunia. She has a copper IUD in place inserted nine months ago. She has had one new sexual partner in the last six weeks and uses no barrier contraception. She is well otherwise, working full time, and would like to keep her IUD. Examination shows lower abdominal tenderness and cervical motion tenderness, with mucopurulent cervical discharge but no peritonism.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, RR 16, T 38.0°C, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Lower abdominal tenderness without guarding, mucopurulent cervix, IUD strings visible, cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative, vaginal swab for chlamydia and gonorrhoea pending, white cell count 13.2 x10^9/L, CRP 42 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Remove the intrauterine device today and commence intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg with oral doxycycline and oral metronidazole 14 days
options.rationale: Routine IUD removal is no longer recommended at PID diagnosis if symptoms are improving within 72 hours of starting antibiotics; removal is reserved for severe disease or non-response. (Source: ASHM: PID; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer all antibiotic therapy until chlamydia and gonorrhoea swab results return in two to three days then treat targeted therapy
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are commenced immediately on clinical diagnosis to prevent tubal damage and infertility; awaiting results risks irreversible complications. (Source: ASHM: PID)

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 14 days as monotherapy with outpatient gynaecology review
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate does not cover Chlamydia, Mycoplasma genitalium or Neisseria gonorrhoeae adequately and is inappropriate as PID monotherapy. (Source: ASHM: PID; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for intravenous gentamicin with intravenous metronidazole and intravenous ampicillin pending swab and culture results
options.rationale: Mild to moderate PID can be treated as an outpatient with oral and intramuscular therapy; intravenous admission is reserved for severe sepsis, pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess or oral therapy failure. (Source: ASHM: PID)

options.text: Give intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg now with oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and metronidazole 400 mg twice daily 14 days
options.rationale: Australian guidance treats PID with the IUD in situ unless severe sepsis, abscess or non-response is present; ceftriaxone covers Neisseria gonorrhoeae, doxycycline covers Chlamydia trachomatis and Mycoplasma genitalium, and metronidazole covers anaerobes. (Source: ASHM: PID; eTG: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: PID is treated on clinical grounds with empirical broad-spectrum cover. The Australian outpatient regimen combines intramuscular ceftriaxone 500 mg single dose, oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days and oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days. The IUD can usually remain in situ provided the woman is reviewed at 72 hours and removal considered if there is no clinical improvement. Hospital admission is indicated for severe disease, pregnancy, tubo-ovarian abscess or failure of oral therapy.
key_points: Leave the IUD in situ and review at 72 hours unless severe disease or non-response.
Ceftriaxone covers gonorrhoea, doxycycline covers chlamydia and mycoplasma, metronidazole covers anaerobes.
Treat empirically without waiting for swab results to prevent tubal damage.
explanation.pearls: Outpatient PID protocol is ceftriaxone 500 mg IM once plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for 14 days, reviewed at 72 hours.

subtopic_detail: Selective fetal growth restriction in MCDA twins
stem: You are the maternal-fetal medicine fellow reviewing a 31-year-old gravida 2 para 1 with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 28+3 weeks. Surveillance ultrasound shows a fetal weight discordance of 28%. Twin A is appropriately grown on the 45th centile, while twin B is on the 4th centile. Liquor volumes are within normal range for both twins. There is no evidence of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome. The umbilical artery Doppler in twin B shows intermittent absent end-diastolic flow.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.8°C
data_table.Examination: Symphysio-fundal height 30 cm, fetal hearts both present and audible, no antepartum bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Estimated fetal weights twin A 1180 g, twin B 850 g, deepest vertical pocket A 4.2 cm, B 3.6 cm, MCA peak systolic velocity both within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Plan delivery now via category 1 caesarean section for both twins to prevent intrauterine death of the smaller twin
options.rationale: Delivery before 32 weeks for type II selective FGR risks preterm morbidity for both twins; close surveillance allows further maturation when the larger twin's status remains reassuring. (Source: ISUOG: Twin pregnancy; RANZCOG: Multiple pregnancy)

options.text: Give antenatal corticosteroids and arrange twice-weekly cardiotocography with weekly Doppler aiming delivery around 32 weeks
options.rationale: Type II selective FGR with intermittent absent end-diastolic flow in twin B requires intensive surveillance, corticosteroid cover and planned delivery between 30 and 32 weeks balancing prematurity risk against in-utero compromise. (Source: ISUOG: Twin pregnancy)

options.text: Proceed to selective laser photocoagulation of placental anastomoses to optimise placental flow to the smaller twin
options.rationale: Laser ablation is established for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome, not standard type II selective FGR without TTTS; selective cord occlusion may be considered in selected severe cases. (Source: ISUOG: Twin pregnancy)

options.text: Switch to fortnightly ultrasound and discharge home without antenatal corticosteroids until at least 34 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Fortnightly surveillance is inadequate for type II selective FGR with absent end-diastolic flow; corticosteroid cover and intensive monitoring are mandatory. (Source: ISUOG: Twin pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend selective fetal reduction of the smaller twin to preserve the survival of the larger appropriately grown twin
options.rationale: Selective reduction in monochorionic pregnancy requires cord occlusion not feticide, and is reserved for severe early type III sFGR or anomalies; type II at 28 weeks does not meet this threshold. (Source: ISUOG: Twin pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Selective fetal growth restriction in monochorionic twins is classified by umbilical artery Doppler in the smaller twin: type I persistently positive, type II persistently absent or reversed, and type III intermittent absent or reversed flow. Type III pregnancies have a 10 to 20% risk of unexpected intrauterine death of the smaller twin and a significant risk of neurological injury to the larger twin if the smaller one dies. Surveillance is intensive with twice-weekly CTG and weekly Doppler. Delivery is usually planned around 32 to 34 weeks under cover of antenatal corticosteroids.
key_points: Type III selective FGR has a 10 to 20% risk of sudden intrauterine death of the smaller twin.
Surveillance is twice-weekly CTG and weekly umbilical artery Doppler.
Antenatal corticosteroids are given when delivery between 24 and 34+6 weeks is anticipated.
explanation.pearls: MCDA twin pair with type III selective FGR and intermittent absent end-diastolic flow is delivered around 32 weeks after a betamethasone course; cord occlusion is considered only in extreme early cases.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to ED to see a 26-year-old woman two days after a medical management of incomplete miscarriage at 9 weeks gestation. She presents with rigors, abdominal pain, foul-smelling discharge and a worsening generalised rash that began on her trunk and is spreading. She has not improved with oral paracetamol at home. She has no relevant past history. On arrival she looks unwell, clammy and confused, with a generalised macular blanching erythematous rash.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 86/48 mmHg, HR 132 bpm, RR 30, SpO2 95% on room air, T 39.8°C, lactate 4.6 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Lower abdominal tenderness, foul-smelling brown vaginal discharge, open internal cervical os, no peritonism, generalised blanching erythroderma
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 x10^9/L, CRP 240 mg/L, creatinine 158 micromol/L, beta-hCG 6800 IU/L, ultrasound shows retained products 28 mm

lead_in: Which combination of interventions provides the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone with metronidazole, intravenous crystalloid resuscitation and elective uterine evacuation on next list
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone-metronidazole alone lacks the antitoxin effect of clindamycin essential for streptococcal toxic shock; deferred evacuation also delays critical source control. (Source: RCOG GTG 64b: Sepsis after pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin and piperacillin-tazobactam, intravenous crystalloid resuscitation and urgent surgical evacuation within 4 hours
options.rationale: Suspected post-miscarriage sepsis with toxic shock features requires broad anaerobic and Gram-positive cover with clindamycin to suppress toxin production, urgent source control by evacuation of the uterus, and aggressive volume resuscitation. (Source: RCOG GTG 64b: Sepsis after pregnancy; eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin monotherapy with intravenous crystalloid and conservative management with serial beta-hCG monitoring
options.rationale: Monotherapy is inadequate for polymicrobial pelvic sepsis and conservative management cannot achieve the rapid source control required by retained products of conception. (Source: RCOG GTG 64b: Sepsis after pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous gentamicin and oral doxycycline with intravenous crystalloid resuscitation and outpatient gynaecology review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Outpatient review in a patient with shock and rising creatinine misses the time-critical resuscitation and evacuation requirement. (Source: RCOG GTG 64b: Sepsis after pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous methylprednisolone and intravenous hydrocortisone bolus to treat presumed adrenal crisis with supportive care
options.rationale: Steroid therapy alone does not address the underlying septic source and may delay definitive antibiotic and surgical management. (Source: RCOG GTG 64b: Sepsis after pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Post-abortal sepsis with toxic shock features is most commonly polymicrobial and may involve invasive group A Streptococcus. The hour-1 bundle of intravenous antibiotics, intravenous crystalloid 30 mL/kg, lactate measurement, blood cultures, urine output monitoring and source control runs in parallel. Clindamycin should be added to anti-streptococcal regimens because it inhibits exotoxin synthesis. Surgical evacuation of retained products of conception is the source control intervention and should occur within 4 hours of presentation in septic shock.
key_points: Add clindamycin to suppress exotoxin in suspected streptococcal toxic shock.
Achieve uterine source control by surgical evacuation within 4 hours in septic shock.
Hour-1 bundle: antibiotics, fluids, lactate, cultures, urine output, source control.
explanation.pearls: In post-miscarriage toxic shock the antitoxin effect of intravenous clindamycin alongside piperacillin-tazobactam plus surgical evacuation within 4 hours offers the best chance of survival.

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 38-year-old man with bipolar I disorder, well-controlled on lithium for the last six years at a stable level. He has had no manic or depressive episodes in three years. He attends today asking for a script for ibuprofen for two weeks for an acute mechanical back strain after lifting at work. He has no past history of renal disease, hypertension or peptic ulceration. He is on no other regular medications and is otherwise well.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, T 36.6°C, weight 84 kg
data_table.Examination: Lumbar paraspinal tenderness only, no neurology, no abdominal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Last lithium level 4 weeks ago: 0.7 mmol/L, eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73 m^2, TSH 2.1 mIU/L, sodium 138 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Prescribe oral celecoxib 200 mg daily as a COX-2 selective alternative without lithium monitoring
options.rationale: COX-2 selective NSAIDs interact with lithium similarly to non-selective NSAIDs and offer no safe-co-prescription advantage in this context. (Source: AMH; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Prescribe oral ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily and recheck lithium level after the two-week course
options.rationale: Co-prescribing an oral NSAID with lithium is generally avoided; checking the level afterwards manages the consequence rather than preventing it. (Source: AMH; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Prescribe oral paracetamol 1 g four times daily and topical diclofenac gel rather than oral NSAID
options.rationale: Oral NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance and can raise lithium levels by 30 to 60%, precipitating toxicity; paracetamol and topical NSAIDs avoid this interaction. (Source: AMH; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Prescribe oral oxycodone 5 mg as required and review back pain weekly for the next month
options.rationale: Opioids for mechanical low back pain risk dependence and falls and are not first-line; non-opioid options that do not interact with lithium are preferred. (Source: AMH; eTG: Pain)

options.text: Withhold lithium for two weeks while the patient uses oral ibuprofen and resume lithium afterwards
options.rationale: Stopping lithium risks relapse of bipolar disorder; abrupt withdrawal also increases the risk of rebound mania within weeks. (Source: AMH; eTG: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index. NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-mediated renal afferent arteriolar dilation, lowering glomerular filtration and lithium clearance. Plasma lithium levels can rise 30 to 60% over 7 to 10 days of NSAID co-administration. Paracetamol with topical NSAIDs is a safer analgesic strategy. If an oral NSAID is unavoidable, the lithium dose is reduced empirically by 25 to 50% and a level checked within 5 to 7 days, alongside close monitoring of renal function and clinical signs of toxicity.
key_points: Oral NSAIDs raise lithium levels by 30 to 60% within 7 to 10 days.
Paracetamol with topical NSAIDs avoids the interaction.
If oral NSAIDs are unavoidable, reduce lithium dose and check level within a week.
explanation.pearls: Co-prescription of an oral NSAID with lithium raises lithium levels by up to 60% within 7 days; if unavoidable, drop the lithium dose by a quarter and check the level at day 5.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar in an early intervention clinic seeing a 19-year-old man with a four-month history of social withdrawal, persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations. He has not used illicit substances and screens negative for cannabis, methamphetamine and synthetic cannabinoids. He is medication-naive and has no relevant past medical history. He is engaged in shared decision-making and is willing to start an antipsychotic but is concerned about weight gain because his older brother gained 18 kg on olanzapine.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/72 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.7°C, BMI 22 kg/m^2
data_table.Examination: Calm, formal thought disorder, persecutory delusions, second-person auditory hallucinations, no insight
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 4.8 mmol/L, total cholesterol 4.2 mmol/L, ECG normal sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms, prolactin within normal range

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial antipsychotic choice?

options.text: Oral clozapine 12.5 mg daily titrated according to protocol with weekly FBC monitoring
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after two adequate trials of other antipsychotics; using it first-line is not supported. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Oral aripiprazole 10 mg daily titrated weekly with monthly metabolic monitoring
options.rationale: Aripiprazole has a lower metabolic burden than olanzapine while maintaining efficacy in first-episode psychosis and aligns with the patient's stated concern. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders; eTG: Psychotropic)

options.text: Oral olanzapine 10 mg daily titrated weekly with monthly metabolic monitoring
options.rationale: Olanzapine is effective but carries the greatest weight-gain risk of the second-generation agents; given this patient's stated concerns it is not the best first choice. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Oral haloperidol 5 mg daily titrated weekly with extrapyramidal symptom monitoring
options.rationale: First-generation antipsychotics have higher rates of EPS and tardive dyskinesia and are not preferred first-line for medication-naive young men with first-episode psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Oral lorazepam 1 mg three times daily as monotherapy until clear diagnosis is established
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychotic symptoms; antipsychotic therapy should not be delayed once first-episode psychosis is diagnosed. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

explanation.summary: RANZCP guidance for first-episode psychosis emphasises early treatment, lower starting doses, shared decision-making and avoidance of olanzapine where metabolic concerns are present. Aripiprazole and risperidone are reasonable first-line options. Baseline weight, waist circumference, fasting glucose, lipids, prolactin and ECG should be measured before starting and reviewed at 3 and 12 months. Long-acting injectables should be discussed early as an option after stabilisation to support adherence.
key_points: Aripiprazole and risperidone are first-line for first-episode psychosis.
Olanzapine has the highest metabolic burden; discuss this explicitly.
Baseline metabolic, cardiac and prolactin screening precedes antipsychotic start.
explanation.pearls: First-episode psychosis starts with low-dose aripiprazole or risperidone, baseline weight, fasting glucose, lipids and ECG, with metabolic review at 3 and 12 months.

stem: You are the GP seeing a 24-year-old woman three months after she was hit by a car as a pedestrian. She continues to have nightmares of the crash, avoids walking on roads, has hypervigilance at intersections and intrusive memories several times a day. She has not been able to return to her university course. She has no past psychiatric history and is not on any medications. She is sexually active and uses a copper IUD for contraception. She drinks no alcohol.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, well-groomed, normal cognition, no thought disorder, denies self-harm or suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: PCL-5 score 52 out of 80 consistent with severe PTSD symptoms, K10 score 32, recent FBC and TFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment recommendation?

options.text: Oral prazosin 1 mg at night for nightmares without other adjunctive psychological or pharmacological therapy
options.rationale: Prazosin is an adjunct for trauma nightmares once first-line therapy is established and is not used as monotherapy for global PTSD symptoms. (Source: ASTSS PTSD guideline)

options.text: Oral sertraline 50 mg daily titrated up to 200 mg daily as the sole initial intervention without therapy
options.rationale: SSRIs are second-line in PTSD after psychological therapies and are added when trauma-focused therapy is unavailable, declined or insufficient. (Source: ASTSS PTSD guideline)

options.text: Oral diazepam 5 mg three times daily for symptomatic control of anxiety and intrusive trauma symptoms
options.rationale: Long-term benzodiazepines worsen PTSD outcomes by impeding fear extinction and are explicitly discouraged. (Source: ASTSS PTSD guideline)

options.text: Trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy or eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Australian guidance places trauma-focused psychological therapies including TF-CBT and EMDR ahead of pharmacotherapy as first-line treatment for adult PTSD with adequate-intensity symptoms. (Source: ASTSS PTSD guideline; RANZCP: PTSD)

options.text: Oral mirtazapine 30 mg at night as initial monotherapy for combined anxiety and sleep symptoms in PTSD
options.rationale: Mirtazapine has limited evidence in PTSD and is not preferred over established SSRIs when pharmacotherapy is selected. (Source: ASTSS PTSD guideline)

explanation.summary: Adult PTSD with adequate-intensity symptoms is treated first-line with trauma-focused psychological therapy, either TF-CBT or EMDR delivered over 8 to 12 sessions. SSRIs (sertraline or paroxetine) are second-line and may be combined with therapy. Benzodiazepines are contraindicated long-term because they impair fear extinction and worsen long-term symptoms. Adjuncts such as prazosin can target persistent trauma nightmares.
key_points: First-line PTSD therapy is TF-CBT or EMDR over 8 to 12 sessions.
Sertraline or paroxetine are second-line when therapy is unavailable or insufficient.
Benzodiazepines worsen PTSD long-term and are avoided.
explanation.pearls: Offer TF-CBT or EMDR as first-line for PTSD with significant symptoms; sertraline 50 mg daily titrating to 200 mg is the second-line pharmacological choice.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 26-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia who started clozapine 18 days ago and is on day 18 of titration at 200 mg daily. The community nurse phones because he reports increasing fatigue, mild central chest discomfort and a low-grade temperature. He has had no respiratory symptoms and his clozapine clinic baseline ECG was normal. He has no past cardiac history. Examination shows sinus tachycardia and mild bibasal crackles.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122 bpm, BP 108/62 mmHg, T 37.6°C, RR 22, SpO2 96% on room air
data_table.Examination: Mild bibasal crackles, no murmurs, no peripheral oedema, JVP not raised
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 380 ng/L (baseline less than 14), CRP 78 mg/L, ECG sinus tachycardia with non-specific T wave changes, FBC unchanged, ferritin within normal range

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Add oral prednisolone 50 mg daily and continue clozapine 200 mg daily with weekly troponin and CRP checks
options.rationale: Steroid therapy does not allow continued clozapine exposure in established myocarditis and there is no evidence base for routine corticosteroid use here. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine)

options.text: Reduce clozapine to 100 mg daily and recheck troponin and CRP in 72 hours as outpatient at the clozapine clinic
options.rationale: Dose reduction does not protect against ongoing myocarditis; continued exposure risks fulminant cardiomyopathy. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine)

options.text: Continue clozapine at 200 mg daily and review with the general practitioner at 7 days for symptom progression
options.rationale: Continuing the offending drug in suspected clozapine myocarditis is dangerous and contradicts the recommended cessation pathway. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine)

options.text: Cease clozapine today, admit for cardiac monitoring and arrange urgent transthoracic echocardiogram and cardiology review
options.rationale: Within the first 4 weeks of clozapine titration any constitutional symptoms with troponin rise, CRP rise or tachycardia mandate immediate clozapine cessation; rechallenge requires specialist cardiology and psychiatry input. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine; AMH)

options.text: Switch to oral olanzapine 20 mg daily and ignore the cardiac investigations pending outpatient psychiatry review
options.rationale: Cardiac workup must continue regardless of antipsychotic switch; ignoring the troponin and CRP rise misses cardiomyopathy. (Source: RANZCP: Clozapine)

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced myocarditis is most common in the first 4 weeks of treatment. Surveillance includes baseline and weekly troponin, CRP and clinical review during titration. A troponin rise above twice the upper limit, a CRP above 100 mg/L, fever, tachycardia disproportionate to dose or any unexplained constitutional symptoms triggers immediate clozapine cessation, cardiology referral and echocardiography. Rechallenge has been described under highly specialised circumstances but is not routine.
key_points: Clozapine myocarditis peaks in the first 4 weeks of titration.
Cease clozapine immediately when troponin rises or constitutional symptoms appear.
Echocardiography and cardiology referral guide diagnosis and prognosis.
explanation.pearls: During clozapine titration any troponin above twice the upper limit, CRP above 100 mg/L or unexplained tachycardia in the first 4 weeks means stop the drug today and call cardiology.

stem: You are the perinatal psychiatry registrar urgently called to the postnatal ward to assess a 30-year-old woman on day 3 after an uncomplicated vaginal birth of her first baby. The midwives report 24 hours of insomnia, pressured speech, religious grandiosity and now disorganised thought. She is breastfeeding and is reluctant to be separated from the baby. She has a family history of bipolar disorder in her sister. She has been medication-naive throughout pregnancy and has had no obstetric complications.

data_table.Examination: Pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose religious delusions, partial insight, no acute medical findings, no fever, no neurology
data_table.Investigations: FBC, urea, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium, TSH all within normal limits, urinalysis clear, no infective focus

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Admit to a general adult psychiatry ward, commence oral diazepam 5 mg three times daily and suspend breastfeeding
options.rationale: General adult wards separate mother and baby unnecessarily; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychosis or mania. (Source: COPE: Perinatal mental health)

options.text: Discharge home with the baby on oral lithium 600 mg daily with paediatric monitoring of the breastfed infant
options.rationale: Outpatient management of postpartum psychosis is unsafe; lithium also has lower breastfeeding compatibility than olanzapine and is rarely first acute choice. (Source: COPE: Perinatal mental health)

options.text: Admit to a mother-baby unit, commence oral olanzapine 5 mg at night and arrange consultant psychiatry review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency requiring inpatient care; olanzapine is compatible with breastfeeding, has rapid antimanic effect and is the preferred first-line acute treatment in this setting. (Source: COPE: Perinatal mental health; RANZCP: Perinatal)

options.text: Arrange community perinatal psychiatry follow-up in 48 hours with no medication changes made today
options.rationale: Deferring treatment for 48 hours in postpartum psychosis exposes both mother and baby to substantial harm including infanticide and maternal suicide risk. (Source: COPE: Perinatal mental health)

options.text: Admit for urgent unilateral electroconvulsive therapy today as the first-line treatment without an oral antipsychotic trial
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in postpartum psychosis but is reserved for severe non-response, catatonia or refusal of oral therapy; oral antipsychotic is the standard first step. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal)

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis affects approximately 1 in 1000 births and clusters in women with bipolar disorder or a family history. Onset is typically within the first 2 weeks postpartum with rapid escalation of insomnia, mood lability, psychosis and risk to mother and baby. Management is psychiatric emergency: admission to a mother-baby unit, antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser where indicated, and ECT for severe or refractory presentations. Olanzapine is preferred for breastfeeding-compatible rapid antimanic effect.
key_points: Postpartum psychosis is an emergency with rapid escalation.
Admit to a mother-baby unit when available; start oral antipsychotic immediately.
Olanzapine is the preferred initial breastfeeding-compatible antipsychotic.
explanation.pearls: Day 3 to 14 postpartum onset of insomnia, mood elevation and delusions is postpartum psychosis until proven otherwise - admit today and start olanzapine 5 to 10 mg at night.
